title: Langton's ant -- pinned display numbers (Computational Curiosity Lab #6)
convention: white -> turn RIGHT (+90 clockwise on a y-down screen), flip to black, step; black -> turn LEFT (-90), flip to white, step. x east, y DOWN; ant starts at the origin facing UP on an all-white grid.

drift.direction_note: sign is convention-dependent: chirality flips x, y-up/down flips y; the magnitude 2*sqrt(2) and period 104 are not.

onset_detector: first step from which the 104-step displacement signature (constant drift + repeating heading) holds for all later steps up to a 20,000-step horizon

signatures._format: [x, y, dir(0=Up,1=Right,2=Down,3=Left), black_count, hash]; hash = polynomial over lexicographically-sorted black cells mod 2147483647

turmites.convention: the i-th letter of the rule-string is the turn on a cell of colour i (colours cycle 0,1,...,n-1,0 as the ant leaves); R = right (+90), L = left (-90)
turmites.langton_note: colour 0 = white -> R, colour 1 = black -> L; same ant as the two-colour rule above
turmites.proven_symmetric_note: rule-strings whose cyclic form splits into LL/RR pairs return to bilateral symmetry infinitely often (Gale-Propp-Sutherland-Troubetzkoy 1995)
turmites.symmetric_examples_first_note: first step at which the coloured field (>= 8 coloured cells) is bilaterally symmetric; the >= 8 floor skips the trivially-symmetric first few steps, so these integers are detector-dependent, not invariants. The CITED invariant is only that symmetry recurs infinitely often.
turmites.chaotic_open_note: grows chaotically; not known whether it ever builds a highway (Wikipedia) -- empirical/open

empirical_catalog.LRRRRRLLR: fills a square region around itself (observation, not a theorem)

checks.note: verify.py prints 'RESULT: 30 checks passed. VERIFICATION PASSED.'; test_core.mjs prints 'RESULT: 19 passed, 0 failed.' The page (header pill, section 8, footer) states the same two counts; test_core.mjs check 19 is the byte-identity guard that the LANGTON_CORE region inlined into index.html matches core.js exactly.

sources: Langton, C. G. (1986). Studying artificial life with cellular automata. Physica D 22, 120-149. doi:10.1016/0167-2789(86)90237-X
Bunimovich, L. A. & Troubetzkoy, S. E. (1992). Recurrence properties of Lorentz lattice gas cellular automata. J. Stat. Phys. 67, 289-302. doi:10.1007/BF01049035
Troubetzkoy, S. (1997). Lewis-Parker lecture: the ant. Alabama J. Math. 21(2).
Gale, D., Propp, J., Sutherland, S. & Troubetzkoy, S. (1995). Further Travels with My Ant. Math. Intelligencer 17(3), 48-56. arXiv:math/9501233
Gajardo, A., Moreira, A. & Goles, E. (2002). Complexity of Langton's ant. Discrete Appl. Math. 117, 41-50. doi:10.1016/S0166-218X(00)00334-6; arXiv:nlin/0306022

Stewart, I. (1994). The ultimate in anty-particles. Sci. Am. 271(1), 104-107. (Origin of the disputed 'Cohen-Kung' name for the unboundedness theorem; the peer-reviewed result of record is Bunimovich-Troubetzkoy 1992.)
